Shias constitute the majority in Iran, Iraq, Azerbaijan, and Bahrain, and a plurality in Lebanon [Source: https://www.cfr.org/article/sunni-shia-divide]. The Iranian Revolution of 1979 and the Iraq War of 2003 brought Sunni-Shia tensions into sharper focus [Source: https://worldhistoryedu.com/sunni-versus-shia-origin-story-of-the-divide/]. The Sunni-Shia divide has been intensified by the increasing politicization of Islam and the rise of fundamentalists, especially amid the Persian Gulf Wars, the U.S.-backed ouster of Saddam Hussein’s Sunni regime in Iraq, and the Arab Spring [Source: https://www.history.com/articles/sunni-shia-divide-islam-muslim].

During the Iran-Iraq War, religion significantly influenced social structures in both countries. In Iraq, the Ba'athist regime attempted to integrate Shia Muslims into the war effort and promoted Islam to counter Iranian religious fervor [Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Iran–Iraq_War]. The war was also fought on religious grounds, with sectarian divisions playing a key role [Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sectarian_violence_in_Iraq]. The strategic use of sectarianism during the war institutionalized sectarian identities, leading to conflicts over status, borders, and power [Source: https://studentreview.hks.harvard.edu/sectarianism-and-conflict-legacies-of-the-iran-iraq-war/].

Key findings regarding the role of religion in the Iran-Iraq War:

*   **Denominations:** Shia Islam predominated in Iran, while Sunni Islam held sway in Iraq. Iranian minority religions also supported the war effort. The conflict intensified the Sunni-Shia divide [Source: https://www.e-ir.info/2016/02/07/the-iran-iraq-war-the-use-of-religion-as-a-tool/, https://www.fletcherforum.org/home/2016/9/13/the-iran-iraq-war-and-sectarianism-in-the-middle-east, https://www.atlanticcouncil.org/blogs/iransource/lessons-from-the-iran-iraq-war-iranian-minorities-won-t-lead-transformative-change/].
*   **Historical Development:** The Shia-Sunni divide has complex historical roots, intensified by the Iranian Revolution (1979) and the Iraq War (2003). The politicization of Islam and the rise of fundamentalism further exacerbated tensions [Source: https://www.cfr.org/article/sunni-shia-divide, https://worldhistoryedu.com/sunni-versus-shia-origin-story-of-the-divide/, https://www.history.com/articles/sunni-shia-divide-islam-muslim].
*   **Influence on Social Structures:** The Ba'athist regime in Iraq attempted to integrate Shia Muslims, while Iran saw energized Shi'ite fundamentalists. Sectarian divisions played a key role, and the strategic use of sectarianism institutionalized identities [Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Iran–Iraq_War, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sectarian_violence_in_Iraq, https://studentreview.hks.harvard.edu/sectarianism-and-conflict-legacies-of-the-iran-iraq-war/].
*   **Ethical Considerations:** Religion motivated individuals to participate in the war, though it didn't initiate it. Iran stated its treatment of Iraqi prisoners of war was based on humanitarian and Islamic considerations [Source: https://oxfordre.com/politics/abstract/10.1093/acrefore/9780190228637.001.0001/acrefore-9780190228637-e-812, https://www.theiranproject.com/en/news/316627/iran%E2%80%99s-treatment-of-iraqi-pows-based-on-humanitarian-islamic-considerations].
*   **Impact of Globalization:** Globalization can erode traditional beliefs, foster new religious movements, and challenge the secularist view that religion will decline [Source: https://moviecultists.com/how-globalization-affects-religious-practices-and-beliefs, https://newheightsboise.com/exploring-religious-traditions-and-meanings/, https://www.academia.edu/68309680/Globalization_Religion_and_Islam].
*   **Role of Religious Institutions:** Religion motivated individuals, and political leaders used religious doctrines and symbolism to legitimize wartime policies [Source: https://oxfordre.com/politics/abstract/10.1093/acrefore/9780190228637.001.0001/acrefore-9780190228637-e-812, https://journals.uvic.ca/index.php/Illumine/article/view/7782/2574, https://www.e-ir.info/pdf/61498].
*   **Relationship with Other Domains**: Each side strategically mixed religion and politics to garner internal allegiance and external support [Source: https://www.e-ir.info/2016/02/07/the-iran-iraq-war-the-use-of-religion-as-a-tool/]. The Iranian leadership painted the war as a battle between believers and unbelievers, effectively mobilizing support [Source: https://oxfordre.com/politics/abstract/10.1093/acrefore/9780190228637.001.0001/acrefore-9780190228637-e-812].
